subsystem: PHP core 'phar' (PHP Archive) extension — the internal iterator callback phar_build(), which is invoked once per element of a user-supplied iterator by Phar::buildFromIterator()/buildFromDirectory(). It resolves each iterator element (a string path, a stream resource, or an SplFileInfo object) to a source file, derives the entry name (str_key) to store it under inside the archive, opens the source, creates the phar entry, copies the file bytes into the phar's temp stream, and records the entry's size and permission flags.
provenance: Identified by PHP/Zend and phar-specific symbols: the zend_object_iterator API (iter->funcs->get_current_data / get_current_key), Zend value macros (Z_TYPE_P, Z_STRVAL_P, Z_STRLEN_P, ZSTR_VAL), Zend exception helpers (zend_throw_exception_ex, spl_ce_UnexpectedValueException/BadMethodCallException), the php_stream_* I/O layer, the phar_entry_data / phar_archive_object types, phar constants (PHAR_MOD, PHAR_UFP, PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK), and helpers phar_get_or_create_entry_data / phar_entry_delref. The banner comment 'sample: primevul_c_train_2425_fixed' and file name train_0060_cwe281.c indicate a PrimeVul-derived sample tied to CWE-281. This matches ext/phar/phar_object.c phar_build() in the PHP source tree.
trust_boundary: The iterator (iter) and every value/key it yields are USER-CONTROLLED: a PHP script passes any Iterator implementation whose get_current_data()/get_current_key() may return arbitrary strings, arbitrary stream resources, or arbitrary SplFileInfo objects. Therefore the file path (fname), the entry key (str_key/str_key_len), the source file contents, and the source file's on-disk stat mode (ssb.sb.st_mode) are all attacker-influenced. The base directory (p_obj->b / p_obj->l) and destination phar (phar_obj->archive) come from the API caller. Hostile inputs: the returned path string (path traversal / open_basedir bypass surface), the returned key string (becomes an archive entry name), and the source file's permission bits which are copied into the archive.

what_the_code_does.explanation: phar_build is a hash-apply callback (returning ZEND_HASH_APPLY_* codes) run for each iterator element. puser is cast to struct _phar_t *p_obj, a context bag carrying the base directory string b and its length l (copied into the uint32_t base_len), the destination class entry c (ce), the phar object p (phar_obj), the result array ret, and the destination stream fp. Note the mixed width/signedness of the length variables: base_len and str_key_len are uint32_t, while fname_len and contents_len are size_t (64-bit on LP64). close_fp defaults to 1 (the source stream will be closed by this function) and is cleared only for the resource case where the caller owns the stream. save and temp are heap pointers freed on every exit path; opened, str_key, data, and fp are set later along specific branches before use.

The callback pulls the current element from the user iterator via the get_current_data function pointer. Because this executes arbitrary user PHP, it can throw; the code checks EG(exception) and bails with STOP. It then guards against a NULL value (a get_current_data that produced nothing), throwing UnexpectedValueException. Both early-outs return before any allocation, so nothing needs freeing here.

The element's zval type selects one of three ingestion strategies. IS_STRING simply breaks out of the switch, deferring filename extraction to lines 130-131 (fname = Z_STRVAL_P(value)); the string is the path of a file to add and its key is derived later from the base directory or the iterator key.

For a resource element the source stream fp is taken from the zval without type verification (php_stream_from_zval_no_verify), then null-checked. Since a stream has no path, the entry name MUST come from the iterator key: the code requires get_current_key to exist, requires the key to be IS_STRING, and requires ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL(Z_STRLEN(key)) to be false (the key must fit in an int) before capturing str_key_len and duplicating the key with estrndup into str_key (also stored in save for later freeing). close_fp is set to 0 because the caller owns the resource, opened is set to the literal "[stream]", and control jumps to after_open_fp, bypassing the base-directory/open_basedir/stream-open logic entirely. The int cast at 'str_key_len = (int)Z_STRLEN(key)' is only safe because of the ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL gate immediately above it.

If the element is an object that is an SplFileInfo instance, the code recovers the underlying spl_filesystem_object via the handlers->offset back-pointer arithmetic. A base directory is mandatory for objects (base_len must be non-zero). For a directory-entry (SPL_FS_DIR) it builds the full path with spprintf('%s%c%s', path, DEFAULT_SLASH, d_name), skips real directories (php_stat FS_IS_DIR), then canonicalizes with expand_filepath; the canonical path replaces fname, its length is recomputed with strlen, and save is set so the buffer is freed later. For SPL_FS_INFO/SPL_FS_FILE it canonicalizes intern->file_name directly. Both success arms jump to phar_spl_fileinfo. The estrndup'd fname is owned via save; note that in this object path save owns a heap buffer, whereas in the plain-string path save stays NULL and fname aliases the zval string.

Any object that is not an SplFileInfo, and any other zval type (array, bool, null, int, etc.), falls through to default and is rejected with an exception. This is the catch-all input-type gate ensuring only the three handled shapes proceed.

Reached only for the IS_STRING case (which broke out of the switch). fname points directly at the interned zval string buffer and fname_len is its length. Because fname aliases the zval, save remains NULL here — the string must NOT be efree'd, and indeed only save/temp are freed later.

When a base directory is configured, the code canonicalizes base into temp (freed later), then requires the source path to lie inside base by testing strstr(fname, base). The entry key is derived by stripping the base prefix: str_key_len = fname_len - base_len and str_key = fname + base_len, then a leading slash/backslash is trimmed. Because str_key_len is uint32_t, the 'str_key_len <= 0' test degenerates to 'str_key_len == 0'; it is intended to reject fname == base but cannot detect a wrap. The strstr check uses substring containment rather than a true prefix comparison, which is a weaker base-directory guarantee than a strncmp against the start of the string.

When no base directory is set, the entry key comes from the iterator key just like the resource case: get_current_key must exist, the key must be an IS_STRING, and it must pass ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL before str_key_len and the estrndup'd str_key (recorded in save) are captured. This is the branch used for plain-string values without a base directory. The same int truncation of Z_STRLEN(key) appears, again guarded by the overflow check above.

For the string / SplFileInfo paths the code enforces open_basedir on fname before opening, then opens the source with php_stream_open_wrapper in binary read mode with STREAM_MUST_SEEK, capturing the resolved name in opened. On failure of either step it frees save/temp and stops. The resource path skipped this block entirely (it jumped to after_open_fp), so a resource stream is not subject to open_basedir here — the caller is trusted to have obtained it legitimately.

The common continuation (also the resource path's landing point) rejects any entry whose key begins with the reserved '.phar' prefix, which is the magic directory phar uses for its manifest/metadata. The length is checked (str_key_len >= 5) BEFORE the memcmp, so the 5-byte compare cannot over-read a shorter str_key. On a match it releases opened, closes fp only if close_fp (i.e. not a caller-owned resource), frees save/temp, and keeps iterating. This is the primary namespace guard preventing an attacker from injecting entries into phar's reserved directory.

The archive entry is created (or fetched) via phar_get_or_create_entry_data using the attacker-derived (str_key, str_key_len) as the entry name inside the phar identified by phar_obj->archive->fname. On failure it reports the entry name and error, frees error/save/temp/opened and closes fp (if owned), then stops. This is where the untrusted key becomes a persistent archive entry name; only the earlier '.phar' prefix filter constrains it.

On success the entry is switched to PHAR_UFP (unmodified-file-pointer) mode: any prior modified stream is closed, offset/offset_abs are anchored at the current position of the phar's underlying stream p_obj->fp, and the source bytes are copied wholesale with php_stream_copy_to_stream_ex, filling contents_len. The compressed/uncompressed sizes are provisionally set to the delta of the stream position. Crucially for CWE-281, the entry's permission flags are then set from the SOURCE file: on stat success, data->internal_file->flags = ssb.sb.st_mode & PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK preserves exactly the source file's permission bits; on stat failure (non-Windows only) it clears the current process umask bits from flags via 'flags &= ~mask'. On Windows the else branch is empty — flags are left at whatever value the entry was created with.

Finally the source stream is closed if owned, the resolved name opened is recorded in the result array p_obj->ret under key str_key (add_assoc_str takes ownership of opened), save/temp heap buffers are freed, and the entry's compressed/uncompressed sizes are overwritten with contents_len (the count returned by the copy). phar_entry_delref drops the extra reference taken by phar_get_or_create_entry_data. The function returns KEEP to continue iterating. Note add_assoc_str copies str_key as the key, so freeing save (which may alias str_key) afterward is safe.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the CWE-281 permission-preservation contract that gives the sample its name. The invariant the implementation must uphold is that a phar entry's stored permission bits must faithfully reflect (and never silently widen beyond) the source file's mode, so that when the archive is later extracted the recreated file is not given more-permissive bits than intended. Two hazards remain in this shown logic: (1) On Windows the else branch is empty (guarded by '#ifndef _WIN32'), so when php_stream_stat fails the entry keeps whatever flags phar_get_or_create_entry_data initialized it with — if that default is a broad mode (e.g. 0666/0777) an attacker who supplies a stat-unfriendly source (or a stream, which never reaches this stat at all) yields an archive entry whose extracted file is world-writable/executable. (2) The stat-success assignment trusts ssb.sb.st_mode & PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK; if PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK is wider than the classic 0777 permission bits, non-permission mode bits (setuid/setgid/sticky) could be carried into the archive. The umask fallback only subtracts umask bits from an existing value and does not establish a safe baseline. Confirm PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK == 0777 (or narrower) and that the entry's default flags before this block are conservative on all platforms.

str_key_len is uint32_t, so 'str_key_len <= 0' is equivalent to 'str_key_len == 0' — the '<' arm is dead. The subtraction fname_len - base_len is done with a size_t (fname_len) minus a uint32_t (base_len); if base_len ever exceeded fname_len the size_t result would be a huge value that truncates into str_key_len and passes this guard, after which str_key = fname + base_len (line 158) would point past the end of fname and the downstream memcmp/entry-creation would read out of bounds (CWE-125/CWE-191). In this code the strstr(fname, base) success on line 147 makes base a substring of fname, so fname_len >= base_len and the wrap cannot occur in practice; hence this is a latent robustness/defense-in-depth concern rather than a live bug. The safe pattern would be an explicit 'if (fname_len <= base_len)' comparison on same-width types before subtracting.

The base-directory containment test uses strstr (substring anywhere) rather than a prefix comparison, while the key is stripped as if base were a strict prefix (str_key = fname + base_len). If base appears as a substring but not at the start of fname, str_key = fname + base_len does not correspond to 'the path relative to base' — it slices at a fixed offset that no longer aligns with the matched substring, producing a corrupted or misleading entry name. Because both canonical paths were run through expand_filepath they normally begin identically, but a crafted fname where base occurs later (e.g. '/x/BASE...' vs base '/BASE') can defeat the intent. The safe invariant is a true prefix check (strncmp(fname, base, base_len) == 0) before deriving the relative key. Impact is limited to wrong entry naming, not memory corruption, so this is low severity.

Z_STRLEN(key) is a size_t but is cast to int and stored in the uint32_t str_key_len, and then estrndup copies exactly str_key_len bytes. If the preceding ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL(Z_STRLEN(key)) gate (lines 56-60 / 190-194) is ever weakened or reordered, a key longer than INT_MAX would truncate to a small/negative int, causing estrndup to copy fewer bytes than the true key while later consumers assume the full length — a classic truncation-driven length mismatch (CWE-197/CWE-681). In the shown code the overflow check immediately precedes and returns early, so the cast is currently safe; the finding is that the safety depends entirely on that gate remaining in place directly above the cast.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if (!(data = phar_get_or_create_entry_data(phar_obj->archive->fname, phar_obj->archive->fname_len, str_key, str_key_len, "w+b", 0, &error, 1))) {
		zend_throw_exception_ex(spl_ce_BadMethodCallException, 0, "Entry %s cannot be created: %s", str_key, error);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: str_key/str_key_len (derived from an attacker-controlled iterator key or path) becomes an archive entry name with only the '.phar' prefix filter (line 238) applied. Entry names containing '..', absolute-path prefixes, or unusual separators are not sanitized here; if phar_get_or_create_entry_data or a later extraction step trusts the stored name to build a filesystem path, this is the entry point for archive path-traversal / zip-slip style issues (CWE-22) at extraction time. The invariant is that phar's entry-name canonicalization must reject traversal sequences and enforce that entries resolve inside the target directory on extraction. This is latent — the bug, if any, lives in phar_get_or_create_entry_data and the extract path, not in this function — but this call is where the untrusted name is committed.

summary: phar_build() ingests fully user-controlled iterator elements (path strings, stream resources, SplFileInfo objects) and turns each into a phar archive entry, copying source bytes and stamping the entry's permission flags from the source file's stat mode. This 'fixed' revision correctly preserves source permissions (CWE-281) via 'flags = st_mode & PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK' with a umask-based fallback, which is the primary contract of interest; residual concerns are the empty Windows stat-failure branch (entry keeps default flags), the uint32_t unsigned subtraction / tautological 'str_key_len <= 0' guard (latent, currently protected by strstr), the substring-vs-prefix base-directory check, and the attacker-controlled entry name flowing into phar_get_or_create_entry_data with only a '.phar' prefix filter. No live memory-corruption bug is evident in the shown code; the size/length gates and the .phar length-before-memcmp are handled correctly.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Callback signature; iter is the untrusted source but carries no fixed buffer of its own — its risk is realized through the values it yields, covered by the value/key anchors.
32-bit unsigned length fields mixed with size_t fname_len in the subtraction at line 148 and the tautological compare at line 150.
Width mismatch with uint32_t base_len in 'fname_len - base_len'; relevant to the underflow/truncation contract at line 148.
Set by php_stream_copy_to_stream_ex on the only path that reads it (line 323); no path reaches the read uninitialized.
Free bookkeeping pointers; every exit path frees them under NULL guards and no path frees the same buffer twice; string-value path keeps save NULL so the aliased zval is never freed.
Set on every path that reaches its use (line 73 for resources, line 222 for files), and consumed by add_assoc_str or released on error; no uninitialized use or leak found.
Result is immediately null-checked (line 37); caller-owned stream, correctly excluded from close via close_fp=0.
This is the mitigating gate that makes the subsequent (int) cast safe; correctly rejects over-INT_MAX keys before truncation.
size_t->int->uint32_t truncation feeding estrndup; safe only because of the adjacent overflow gate — flagged as gate-dependent.
Standard Zend object-recovery idiom; instanceof_function guard on line 76 ensures the object really is an spl_filesystem_object.
spprintf allocates to fit; not a fixed-size buffer copy, no overflow. fname freed via efree/save.
Unsigned subtraction across size_t/uint32_t; underflow/truncation primitive, latent (guarded by strstr containment).
Tautological on unsigned uint32_t (equivalent to ==0); cannot detect a wrapped subtraction — reported with line 148.
Substring test used where a prefix test is intended; weak containment guarantee that can misalign the derived relative key.
Pointer/length arithmetic covered by the line-148/150/147 findings; the '/'||'\\' trim reads one in-bounds byte (str_key_len>0 already ensured).
Present and enforced for file/string paths before opening; correct validation gate. Resource path intentionally bypasses it (caller-owned stream).
Return value null-checked (line 224) with proper cleanup; opens read-only.
Length checked BEFORE the 5-byte memcmp, so no over-read; correctly bounds the reserved-directory guard.
Attacker-controlled entry name committed to the archive with only the '.phar' filter; latent path-traversal-at-extraction surface (CWE-22) in the callee.
The core CWE-281 permission-preservation contract; residual risk on the empty Windows stat-failure branch and on PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK width.
Bulk copy into the phar's own growable stream; no fixed destination buffer, size returned in contents_len.
Key str_key is copied by add_assoc_str and opened ownership transfers cleanly; subsequent efree(save) does not create a UAF.

findings.title: Archive entry permission bits taken from source stat with an empty Windows / stream fallback (improper permission preservation)
findings.anchor: if (php_stream_stat(fp, &ssb) != -1) { data->internal_file->flags = ssb.sb.st_mode & PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK ; } else { #ifndef _WIN32 ... umask ... #endif }
findings.bug_class: Improper preservation of permissions (permission bits set from untrusted source mode; weak fallback)
findings.invariant: A phar entry's stored permission flags must equal the source file's real permission bits masked to the classic permission set (<=0777, no setuid/setgid/sticky), and when the source cannot be stat'd (including the resource/stream path that never reaches this stat) the entry must default to a conservative, umask-respecting mode on EVERY platform — never leave the entry at a broad initialized default that extraction would recreate as world-writable/executable.
findings.failure_mode: On Windows the else branch is compiled out (#ifndef _WIN32), so a stat failure leaves data->internal_file->flags at whatever value the entry was created with; if that default is permissive, extracting the phar produces an over-permissioned file. Stream-resource inputs bypass this block entirely (they jump to after_open_fp before the copy/stat, and the flags are only touched here). If PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK admits non-permission mode bits, a source file with setuid/setgid/sticky bits carries those into the archive. In all cases the attacker controls the source file's mode, so a chosen source can steer the archived entry's permissions.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK is 0777 (or narrower); confirm the initial value of data->internal_file->flags after phar_get_or_create_entry_data is conservative on Windows and on the stream path; verify that the stat-failure default cannot yield world-writable/executable extracted files.

findings.title: Unsigned uint32_t entry-length subtraction with a tautological non-positive guard
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer underflow / signed-style comparison on unsigned type
findings.invariant: The relative-key length must be computed only after proving fname_len > base_len using same-width types; str_key_len must never be assigned a wrapped value, and the guard must reject fname_len <= base_len rather than only fname_len == base_len.
findings.failure_mode: str_key_len is uint32_t and fname_len is size_t, so 'fname_len - base_len' with base_len > fname_len would produce a large value that truncates into str_key_len and slips past 'str_key_len <= 0' (which for an unsigned type is only true at exactly 0). str_key = fname + base_len (line 158) would then point past fname's end and downstream reads (memcmp at 238, entry creation at 259) would be out of bounds. The strstr(fname, base) success on line 147 currently guarantees fname_len >= base_len, so the wrap is not reachable here — this is a latent defense-in-depth defect that becomes live if the base check is ever changed.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify base is always a genuine prefix of fname when reaching line 148, and replace the guard with an explicit same-width 'if (fname_len <= base_len)' check performed before the subtraction.

findings.title: Base-directory containment uses substring (strstr) instead of a prefix comparison
findings.invariant: To treat base as the directory the entry is relative to, the code must verify base is a true PREFIX of fname (e.g. strncmp(fname, base, base_len) == 0) before slicing str_key at offset base_len; substring containment does not justify the fixed-offset slice.
findings.failure_mode: strstr matches base anywhere inside fname, but str_key = fname + base_len assumes base sits at the very start. A path where the canonical base string appears as a non-leading substring passes the guard yet yields a relative key sliced at the wrong offset, producing a corrupted or misleading archive entry name. Both operands are expand_filepath-canonicalized, which normally forces a shared leading prefix, so exploitability is limited to naming confusion rather than memory safety.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm expand_filepath guarantees fname begins with base for in-directory files; otherwise switch to a prefix comparison to avoid mis-sliced entry names.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled iterator key/path becomes an archive entry name with only a '.phar' prefix filter
findings.bug_class: Unsanitized archive entry name (path traversal at extraction)
findings.invariant: Entry names committed to the archive must be canonicalized/validated so that '..', absolute prefixes and stray separators cannot cause a later extraction to write outside the intended directory; the '.phar' prefix filter alone is not sufficient traversal protection.
findings.failure_mode: str_key is derived either from the raw iterator key (estrndup of Z_STRVAL(key)) or from the path suffix after base, and reaches phar_get_or_create_entry_data as the entry name with only the line-238 '.phar' filter applied. If the callee or the extraction routine trusts this name to build a filesystem path, an entry name containing traversal sequences yields a zip-slip-style write outside the target on extraction. The bug, if present, lives in phar_get_or_create_entry_data and the extractor, not in phar_build itself.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify phar_get_or_create_entry_data (and the phar extraction path) canonicalize/reject entry names containing '..' or absolute components so stored names cannot escape the extraction directory.

audit_checklist: Confirm PHAR_ENT_PERM_MASK is 0777 (or narrower) and strips setuid/setgid/sticky, so source mode bits copied at line 298 cannot import dangerous non-permission bits.

Confirm the initial value of data->internal_file->flags after phar_get_or_create_entry_data is conservative on Windows and for stream-resource inputs, since those paths never execute the umask fallback (empty #ifndef branch).

Verify base is always a true prefix of fname when line 148 runs, and replace 'str_key_len = fname_len - base_len' + 'str_key_len <= 0' with a same-width 'if (fname_len <= base_len)' guard and a strncmp-based containment check.
Confirm the ZEND_SIZE_T_INT_OVFL gate stays immediately above every '(int)Z_STRLEN(key)' truncation (lines 62 and 196) so estrndup can never copy a truncated length.
Trace str_key into phar_get_or_create_entry_data and the extraction routine to ensure entry names with '..'/absolute components are rejected (no zip-slip on extraction).

Audit every efree(save)/efree(temp)/zend_string_release(opened)/php_stream_close(fp) exit path for double-free or leak, especially the interaction between the string-value path (save == NULL, fname aliases the zval) and the object/resource paths (save owns a heap buffer).
Verify contents_len is always initialized before line 323 on every path that reaches the success epilogue, including the resource path that jumps to after_open_fp.
